title: Stacks and Queues
learningObjectives: Explain Stacks and Queues with interview-grade clarity
Implement Stacks and Queues solutions in clean Java
Choose the right pattern and justify complexity
Handle boundary cases without brittle logic
Use this topic to solve coding-round problems confidently

sections.title: Why Stacks and Queues matters
sections.content:
Stacks and queues help model constrained processing order: LIFO for undo-style flow and FIFO for scheduling pipelines.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Monotonic patterns, queue/stack emulation, and boundary handling.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 22, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Stacks and Queues
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Stacks and queues help model constrained processing order: LIFO for undo-style flow and FIFO for scheduling pipelines.

### Core interview mental model
For Stacks and Queues, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Stacks and Queues are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Stack = LIFO** — parentheses, DFS iterative, monotonic stack for next-greater-element.

**Queue = FIFO** — BFS levels, task scheduling; `Deque` for both ends (`ArrayDeque` preferred over `Stack` class).

**PriorityQueue** is heap-backed — O(log n) insert; not a stable queue for fairness unless you tie-break.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Stack (Deque): LIFO push/pop for bracket matching.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Queue: FIFO with ArrayDeque — task scheduling order.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Monotonic deque: sliding window maximum pattern (sketch).

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using Stack class blindly instead of modern Deque.
Forgetting to pop stale elements in monotonic structures.
Confusing stack top with queue front/back semantics.
Not proving amortized complexity for repeated push/pop patterns.

sections.title: Exercise - Stacks and Queues Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Stacks and Queues**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a stack (LIFO)?
conceptual.answer: Push/pop at one end—call stacks, undo, DFS.

codeBased.question: Sliding window maximum with deque.
codeBased.answer: Remove smaller indices from back when new larger arrives; pop front if out of window.

wrongAnswers: Queue always means FIFO in every Java class named Queue.
PriorityQueue guarantees FIFO among equal priorities.
java.util.Stack is the preferred stack implementation today.
ArrayDeque cannot be used as a LIFO stack.
BFS always uses a stack data structure.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Day 22 Focus | Quick Reminder | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Stacks and Queues | Choose pattern before coding | Random brute-force wandering |
| Correctness | Invariant + edge checks | Dry-run once | Off-by-one / null-state miss |
| Complexity | Derive, do not guess | Mention time + space | Incomplete complexity answers |
| Communication | Explain decisions | Use trade-off statement | Generic textbook responses |
| Quality | Readable Java methods | Stable naming + small units | Clever but fragile logic |
